subject.title: English B
subject.description:
**IB English B** is a **Group 2** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **language acquisition through five prescribed themes: identities, experiences, human ingenuity, social organization, and sharing the planet**, with a strong focus on **developing receptive, productive, and interactive communication skills in English as a second language**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| **Assessment** | **Format** | **SL** | **HL** | **Weighting** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Paper 1** | Productive skills - writing | 1 hour | 1 hour 15 minutes | 25% |
| **Paper 2** | Receptive skills - listening and reading comprehension | 1 hour 45 minutes | 2 hours | 50% |
| **Individual Oral** | Internal assessment - oral communication | 15 min prep + 10 min exam | 15 min prep + 10 min exam | 25% |

strengths: Clear, sustained focus on legal and social constraints on womenâs self-sufficiency
Explicit, well-linked research question restated across abstract, introduction, and headings
Logical organization into labeled subsections with primary and secondary sources
weaknesses: Methodology lacks explicit criteria for source selection and justification
Minor verbosity and occasional digressions into general Regency background
Insufficient explanation of why certain non-scholarly sources were chosen
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The work demonstrates a consistently clear and appropriate focus, and the research question is precisely stated and effectively connected to the discussion. Methodology is outlined with logical structure and use of primary/secondary materials, but the absence of explicit selection criteria and some reliance on lower-quality sources prevents full marks. Overall performance aligns with the 5-6 band, meriting a score of 5.

strengths: Clear and coherent subject knowledge of Regency social hierarchy, entailment, and coverture
Appropriate use of key literary and socio-historical terminology
Effective integration of primary quotations to support context
weaknesses: Overreliance on SparkNotes and popular websites limits depth of application
Occasional factual inaccuracies (e.g., incorrect publication dates and periodization)
Use of dictionary definitions for legal concepts adds little analytical value
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student exhibits organized and mostly coherent knowledge, correctly applying terminology and concepts. However, the uneven quality and relevance of sources, coupled with a few factual slips, restrict the work to the top of the 3â4 band. Thus a score of 4 reflects mostly relevant application of material with room to strengthen scholarly rigor.

strengths: Consistent relevance of primary and secondary evidence to the research question
Insightful analysis linking legal concepts (entailment, coverture) to narrative consequences
Coherent, focused arguments with clear evaluation of Elizabethâs and Charlotteâs contrasting experiences
weaknesses: Dependence on non-academic web sources reduces scholarly breadth
Limited engagement with counter-interpretations or critical diversity
Some paragraphs drift toward plot summary rather than sustained critical analysis
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The investigation exhibits predominantly excellent critical thinking: research is carefully chosen and clearly relevant, analysis is insightful in tracing thematic connections, and the discussion is focused and coherent. Minor lapses in source credibility assessment and deeper critical diversity place it just below the topmost band, warranting 11 out of 12.

strengths: Structure fully adheres to required EE format with comprehensive table of contents
Clear section headings that guide the reader through argument flow
Title page, abstract, and bibliography correctly formatted
weaknesses: Inconsistent citation formatting in some passages
Page numbering placement varies across sections
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The presentation is clearly appropriate and complete. All essential elementsâthe title page, abstract, contents, main sections, conclusion, and bibliographyâare in place, and section headings aid navigation. Minor formatting inconsistencies do not impede readability, meriting the maximum score of 4.

strandSelections.reasoning: The research question is explicitly stated on the title page and restated in the abstract and introduction.  Although wordy, it is precise in scope (depiction of legal and social limits on womenâs self-sufficiency in Pride and Prejudice) and is consistently linked to discussion in every main section heading.  Occasional widening into general Regency background does not blur the central focus, so the clarity and connection remain strong.

The student analyses how specific laws (entailment, coverture) surface in the narrative and links quotations to gender constraints.  Yet paragraphs often drift into plot summary, and counter-arguments or nuanced readings are sparse.  The argument is coherent but lacks the sustained, tightly integrated analysis demanded for the higher bands.

Quotations from Austen are appropriately chosen to illustrate entailment, coverture and marriage pressures, and some historical commentary is woven in.  Nonetheless, a sizeable proportion of references stem from SparkNotes summaries or popular web articles that add little analytical depth.  Application is therefore âmostly relevantâ rather than consistently rigorous.

Essential layout elements are present: title, word count, table of contents and bibliography.  Some citation formatting is irregular, and page numbering is not visible in the extract, but the majority of layout conventions are met, justifying a secure score within the 3-4 band.

The student shows clear knowledge of Regency social hierarchy, entailment and coverture, and understands Austenâs use of irony and satire.  Occasional factual slips (e.g., dating the setting to 1814-1837) and broad generalisations limit coherence, but overall the knowledge conveyed is organised and supported, meriting the top of the 3-4 band.

The essay follows the required EE structure (title page, abstract, contents page, introduction, main chapters, conclusion, bibliography).  Section headings are clear and aid navigation.  Minor formatting inconsistencies (e.g., Roman numerals vs. bullet points) do not disrupt overall clarity, so the structure is âclearly appropriate and complete.â

The investigation draws on a fair spread of primary evidence and secondary commentary, and most selected material bears clear relevance to the research question.  However, dependence on non-academic web sources and limited critical diversity reduce scholarly breadth, placing the work solidly in the âgoodâ rather than âexcellentâ band.

The student states a focused topic â the impact of law and social conventions on womenâs self-sufficiency in Pride and Prejudice â and sustains this purpose throughout the abstract, introduction, section headings and conclusion. The language repeatedly returns to the key ideas of âlimitations,â âself-sufficiencyâ and â19th-century British society,â signalling a clear and appropriate focus.  Minor verbosity and some digressions are present, yet overall the reader can quickly grasp what the investigation will cover and why it matters.

A logical line of reasoning runs from historical context through textual examples to conclusions about womenâs restricted self-sufficiency.  Evaluation of evidence is presentâe.g., contrasting Elizabeth and Charlotteâbut tends to be asserted rather than critically weighed.  Occasional repetition and limited engagement with opposing interpretations keep the discussion within the middle of the 4-6 range.

The essay sets out its method (primary novel plus historical/critical secondary sources) and organises material into logical, labelled subsections.  A bibliography and in-text citations are provided.  However, the range of supporting texts is uneven: SparkNotes, blogs and generalist websites appear beside scholarly works, and there is no explicit explanation of criteria for source selection.  These weaknesses reduce the sense of âeffective and informedâ choice, placing the work in the upper-middle band.

Literary and socio-historical terms such as âentailment,â âprimogeniture,â âcoverture,â âmotif,â and ânovel of mannersâ are used correctly and repeatedly.  Minor lapses (e.g., âmasculine superiority complex behaviorâ) occur, but terminology is generally accurate and consistent across the essay.

annotations.comment: The title clearly communicates the focused investigation into legal and social constraints on womenâs self-sufficiency, establishing purpose and scope.
Word count is provided at the start and remains within the 4 000-word limit, enhancing compliance with formatting requirements.
Page numbering is not visible on this title page; ensure consistent placement of page numbers in all sections.
The abstract describes the study broadly but lacks explicit criteria for selecting and evaluating sources; clarify the methodology design.
The second paragraph of the abstract drifts into contextual background and methodology details without linking back to the research question; streamline for focus.
Abstract word count is noted (224 words), demonstrating adherence to word-count reporting conventions.
The table of contents is comprehensive and clearly laid out, aiding navigation through all major sections.
Several table cells appear misaligned, which disrupts readability; refine the cell formatting to present the contents uniformly.
The opening quotation in the introduction is not fully contextualized or accurately cited; include a precise citation and discuss its relevance.
The introduction includes detailed publication history of the novel, which detracts from the immediate research focus; tighten to foreground the research question.
Appropriate use of the term ânovel of mannersâ demonstrates subject familiarity and academic terminology.
There is confusion between Regency and Victorian eras in the introduction; verify and correct historical periodization for accuracy.
The stated setting of 1814â1837 contradicts the novelâs publication in 1813; adjust the timeline or justify its relevance.
The analysis relies heavily on SparkNotes and non-academic websites; integrate peer-reviewed sources to enhance scholarly rigor.
The discussion of class and money as structuring forces exhibits clear subject knowledge of the novelâs social context.
Footnote citations vary in style and completeness; adopt a consistent referencing format throughout the essay.
Methodology rationale is implicit but not detailed; explicitly outline criteria for source selection to strengthen Focus and Method.
The date â1976â attributed to Blackstoneâs coverture is historically incorrect; verify the original publication date (1765â1769).
Quoting Mrs. Bennetâs satirical remark on entailment effectively illustrates the lawâs narrative impact.
Analysis of Elizabethâs cynical response to Darcyâs description of an âaccomplished womanâ insightfully demonstrates Austenâs critique of gender norms.
Reliance on dictionary definitions for concepts adds little analytical value; consult legal or historical scholarship for depth.
Deepen the evaluation by contrasting Austenâs portrayal with at least one scholarly counter-interpretation to enrich the discussion.
Dictionary-based definitions for coverture and self-sufficiency limit the depth of analysis; integrate historical legal commentary.
Linking the legal concept of coverture directly to the narrative consequences in the novel shows sound research quality.
When using web-based sources, briefly assess their credibility to demonstrate informed selection and strengthen research quality.
Good selection of the opening line quotation highlights societal expectations and effectively grounds the analysis in the primary text.
